transcript:
[doctor] hey angela how are you
[patient] i'm good how are you
[doctor] i'm good so i see we are here you had a recent diagnosis of stage three nonsmol cell lung cancer and so we are following up on your neo adevant chemotherapy so you can you tell me how you're doing today
[patient] yeah you know i'm i'm doing okay i have a little bit of some shortness of breath that i noticed over the weekend and you know i i'm i considered myself pretty active before this i would walk my dog around the block and now i i ca n't even walk you know down the street without being short of breath
[doctor] okay yeah yeah i mean that's that's definitely understandable it says you had a four point four centimeter left upper lobe nodule in there so it's kinda big but that's understandable and we're doing the chemo to try to reduce that that that tumor that you do have so how often do you normally run with your dogs
[patient] i mean i would i would take them for i would normally take them for like mile long walks everyday and just this weekend i could only walk down to the end of the street i had to have my husband take the dog fit walk
[doctor] man okay what kind of dog do you have
[patient] i have a dobrman
[doctor] yeah they are pretty active that makes sense alright so have you been coughing have you had any phlegm
[patient] i've been having a dry cough no phlegm
[doctor] okay have you noticed any fever
[patient] no fever
[doctor] no fever alright what about any other symptoms like any body aches any sore throat sinus pain
[patient] well i mean now that you mentioned it like every time i swallow it hurts it does n't feel like i have a cold because it hurts down here more in my chest but every time i swallow it it's really painful
[doctor] okay so when you are swallowing i know some of my patients feel this do you feel like the food is getting stuck
[patient] no the food is not getting stuck it just hurts
[doctor] okay what about when you're you're taking liquids are you able to take in both hydrocort liquids
[patient] i mean i definitely feel like over the weekend i have n't been taking it as much because it hurt so much but i i am able to i am able to drink and i've been taking ensure and things like that i had a milk shake
[doctor] okay that's that's that's that's good yeah we we definitely want you to to take in as much liquid as possible best especially since you're on the chemotherapy and the radiation just so you wo n't get dehydrated because that will make you feel a lot worse if you are dehydrated okay so on a on a mental emotional standpoint i know you said you have your husband with you how you doing with that you have the support that you need you need to speak to me though
[patient] it's very helpful you know as you can understand i'm just like devastated when i got this diagnosis and i'm just i'm just trying to figure everything out and but he is very good and my daughter is is very helpful too and i you know i think for the most part i'm very fortunate to have them
[doctor] okay so how how are you feeling on your chemo see you're on a combination of sysplatin and a topicide you had your last dose a few days ago any nausea any fatigue from that
[patient] i was a little nauseated and tired but i took the zofran that you prescribed and that seemed to help and you know i i got some rest it's it's it's been okay
[doctor] okay that's good so let me do a quick physical exam on you just gon na check your vitals here so your vitals are normal your pulse ox is okay so everything's good with that so i'm gon na feel your neck on your neck exam i do n't appreciate any cervical lymphadenopathy no subclavicular adenopathy your heart exam regular rate and rhythm no murmur your lung exam so i do notice some crackles in your lungs bilaterally that's just a faint sound we hear when you take a deep breath so that could mean you have some fluid in there and that is to be expected due to your lung cancer diagnosis so i'm gon na look at your skin exam so on your skin exam on your chest i do see some erythema on the anterior aspect of your chest on the left side and that could be due to that radiation sometimes patients do get a radiation burn and it's it'll get better with time especially once you're done with the the radiation treatment and on your extremity it there is no lower extremity edema in your legs so prior to coming to see me you did get a chest x-ray it looks like you do have mild radiation umitis that means you have inflammation of your lungs due to the radiation so let's talk a little bit about my assessment and plan for you so your first diagnosis you know is is the lung cancer so we are gon na continue you on that chemo regimen on that you do have continue on that that current radiation doses i think you're on forty five grade yep you're on that and then when the when the regimen's complete we'll do some more testing and hopefully we can see a resection of that tumor so that has just gotten little smaller and we can go inside and and take it out so for your second diagnosis the radiation pneumonitis i'm gon na prescribe you a low dose steroid prednisone forty milligrams one tablet a day for five days have you been on on a steroids before
[patient] no i've only ever heard bad things about them they make people jittery and gain weight
[doctor] i mean and and i for you that's that that's for patients that you know who are on them for a a long period of time right you're only gon na be on it for five days that should n't affect you too bad you might feel when you coming off of it after the five days a few muscle aches other than that you should be fine you should n't really experience much weight gain other other than not not much at all since you're only on on it for five days so but that's gon na help you reduce some of the inflammation in your lungs help you breathe better so we can continue the radiation treatment because we definitely need to do that because our most important thing right now is to try to decrease the size of that tumor okay and then for your third issue the oddy ophagia or it come terms the painful swallowing right you said you had that from the from the radiation so that's from the inflammation of your lungs that's kinda creeping up to your esophagus that's why you're you're feeling that pain so i'm gon na prescribe you a lidocaine squishy and swallow you can do that four times a day and so you'll do it and you'll be able to eat immediately afterwards because it would do some of that pain free from swallowing and that will help you taking liquids and food because we definitely need you well off it and and and hydrated while you're going through this cancer therapy okay
[patient] okay
[doctor] alright so do you have any questions for me
[patient] no
[doctor] alright so we will get you those medications those prescriptions and then we will see you back in two weeks
[patient] okay great thank you
[doctor] thanks
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Follow-up of stage III non-small cell lung cancer.

MEDICAL HISTORY

Patient reports recent diagnosis of stage III non-small cell lung cancer.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient reports she was previously active taking mile long walks with her doberman. She states her husband is her support system and has been helpful with this new diagnosis. Her daughter has also been helpful.

MEDICATIONS

Patient reports receiving chemotherapy consisting of Cisplatin and etoposide. She is also taking Zofran.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports fatigue. Denies fever.
HENT: Reports odynophagia.
Respiratory: Reports dyspnea and dry cough.
Gastrointestinal: Reports nausea.

VITALS

Vitals are normal including pulse oximetry.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Neck
- General Examination: Neck is supple without cervical lymphadenopathy, or subclavicular adenopathy.

Respiratory
- Auscultation of Lungs: Rales in the lungs bilaterally.

Cardiovascular
- Auscultation of Heart: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.

Musculoskeletal
- Examination: No edema.

Integumentary
- Examination: Some erythema on the left anterior aspect of the chest.

Hematology/Lymphatic/Immunology
- Palpation: No enlarged lymph nodes.

RESULTS

Pathology report is reviewed and shows evidence of a 4.4 cm left upper lobe nodule.

Chest x-ray is reviewed and shows evidence of mild radiation pneumonitis.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage III non-small cell lung cancer.
- Medical Reasoning: This is a new diagnosis for this patient. Recent pathology report showed evidence of a 4.4 cm left upper lobe nodule.
- Patient Education and Counseling: We discussed that once her treatment regimen is complete we will proceed with further testing and hopefully tumor resection.
- Medical Treatment: Patient will continue on current regimen consisting of Cisplatin and etoposide. Her radiation will remain at 45 Gy.

2. Radiation pneumonitis.
- Medical Reasoning: Recent chest x-ray showed evidence of mild radiation pneumonitis.
- Patient Education and Counseling: We discussed she is experiencing inflammation of the lungs secondary to her radiation treatment. The risks and benefits of prednisone were also discussed.
- Medical treatment: A prescription for prednisone 40 mg 1 tablet a day for 5 days was provided.

3. Odynophagia.
- Medical Reasoning: Patient reports symptoms of painful swallowing.
- Patient Education and Counseling: I advised her that her radiation pneumonitis is also affecting her esophagus which is resulting in her painful swallowing. We discussed the importance of nutrition and hydration while undergoing chemotherapy.
- Medical treatment: Prescription for lidocaine viscous solution to be completed 4 times a day was provided.
Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

INSTRUCTIONS

Patient will return for follow-up in 2 weeks.
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old female with a recent diagnosis of stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer, presents for follow-up on her neoadjuvant chemotherapy regimen. She reports shortness of breath, which she attributes to her decreased physical activity since her diagnosis. She normally walks her dog for a mile-long walk daily, but now she can only walk down to the end of the street without becoming short of breath. She denies coughing or phlegm, but admits to a dry cough. She also reports pain with swallowing, which she describes as a sharp pain in her chest that worsens when she swallows. She has been taking Ensure and other liquids, but has been having trouble taking her hydrocortisone liquids due to the pain. She is on a combination of cisplatin and topotecan chemotherapy, which she started a few days ago.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer, diagnosed recently.

Review of systems: The patient reports shortness of breath, dry cough, and painful swallowing.

Current medications: Cisplatin, topotecan, zofran, and hydrocortisone.

Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's pulse, oxygen saturation, and temperature are within normal limits.

Physical exam: The patient's physical exam is notable for crackles in the lungs bilaterally, which could indicate fluid in the lungs, consistent with her lung cancer diagnosis. The skin exam reveals erythema on the anterior aspect of the chest on the left side, which could be due to radiation burns. The extremity exam shows no lower extremity edema.

Test Results: The patient had a recent chest X-ray, which showed mild radiation pneumonitis, indicating inflammation of the lungs due to radiation.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer and radiation pneumonitis, which is causing her symptoms of shortness of breath, dry cough, and painful swallowing.

Problem list: Nonsmall cell lung cancer; radiation pneumonitis; painful swallowing.

Plan:
I prescribed prednisone 40 mg once daily for 5 days to reduce inflammation in the lungs and alleviate symptoms. I also prescribed lidocaine spray to help alleviate painful swallowing, which can be used 4 times a day. I will have the patient take this medication after eating and drinking to help her stay hydrated.

Follow-up: We will see the patient back in two weeks to monitor her progress and adjust her treatment plan as needed.

Subjective:
The patient, a 65-year-old woman with a history of stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer, presented with shortness of breath, which she describes as a new symptom that started over the weekend. She reports being unable to walk her dog as far as she used to without feeling short of breath. She denies coughing, phlegm, fever, body aches, sore throat, and sinus pain. However, she reports a painful sensation in her chest when swallowing, which is not associated with food getting stuck. She has been able to drink liquids, including Ensure and milkshakes, but has been taking them less frequently due to the pain. She is on a combination of cisplatin and paclitaxel chemotherapy, which she started a few days ago, and has been experiencing nausea and fatigue, which have been managed with zofran.

Past medical history:
The patient has stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer, for which she is undergoing chemotherapy and radiation.

Review of systems:
The patient reports a dry cough, shortness of breath, and painful swallowing, but denies other symptoms.

Current medications:
Cisplatin, paclitaxel, zofran, prednisone 40mg daily for 5 days, lidocaine spray for swallowing pain.

Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's pulse, blood pressure, oxygen saturation, and temperature are within normal limits.

Physical exam:
The patient's physical exam is notable for crackles in the lungs bilaterally, which may indicate fluid in the lungs, and erythema on the anterior aspect of the chest on the left side, which may be due to radiation. The patient's skin exam is also notable for no lower extremity edema.

Test results:
The patient had a chest x-ray that showed mild radiation pneumonitis, which is inflammation of the lungs due to radiation.

Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer; radiation pneumonitis; and painful swallowing due to radiation-induced inflammation of the esophagus.

Plan:
I will continue the patient on her current chemotherapy regimen and radiation doses. I will prescribe prednisone 40mg daily for 5 days to reduce inflammation in her lungs and help her breathe better. I will also prescribe lidocaine spray for swallowing pain, which she can use 4 times a day. The patient will be seen again in two weeks.

Follow-up:
The patient will be seen again in two weeks to assess the effectiveness of the current treatment plan and to make any necessary adjustments.

HPI: The patient, a female with a recent diagnosis of stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer, presents with shortness of breath, which she describes as a new symptom that began over the weekend. She reports that she was previously able to walk her dog for a mile, but now can only walk down the street without experiencing shortness of breath. She also complains of a dry cough, but no phlegm or fever. Additionally, she reports that swallowing hurts, particularly in her chest, but the food is not getting stuck. She has been taking Ensure and other liquids, but has decreased her intake due to pain. She is currently on a combination of cisplatin and topotecan chemotherapy and has experienced some nausea and fatigue, but has been able to manage these symptoms with Zofran and rest.

Assessment: The patient has stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer, radiation pneumonitis, and dysphagia.

I will prescribe prednisone 40mg once daily for five days to reduce inflammation in her lungs, and lidocaine spray for painful swallowing. I will also continue to monitor her symptoms and adjust treatment as necessary.

Follow-up: The patient will return in two weeks for a follow-up appointment.

Subjective:
The patient is a 45-year-old woman who was recently diagnosed with stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer and is currently undergoing neo-adjuvant chemotherapy. She reports a decrease in physical activity due to shortness of breath, which she describes as a new symptom that started over the weekend. She mentions that she was previously able to walk her dog for a mile, but now has to stop after a short distance. She also reports a dry cough, no phlegm, and no fever. Additionally, she experiences pain when swallowing, which she describes as a sharp pain in her chest, but does not feel like food is getting stuck. She has been taking Ensure and other liquids to stay hydrated, but has been unable to take in as much liquid as she normally would due to the pain. She has a supportive husband and daughter who are helping her cope with her diagnosis.

The patient has a recent diagnosis of stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer, for which she is undergoing neo-adjuvant chemotherapy.

Review of systems:
The patient reports shortness of breath, dry cough, no phlegm, no fever, and painful swallowing.

Current medications:
I prescribed zofran [dosage not mentioned] [frequency not mentioned] for nausea.

Physical exam: The patient's physical exam revealed no cervical lymphadenopathy, subclavicular adenopathy, or heart murmur. However, the lung exam revealed crackles bilaterally, which could indicate fluid in the lungs. The skin exam revealed erythema on the anterior aspect of the chest on the left side, which could be due to radiation burn. The extremity exam revealed no lower extremity edema in the legs.

Test Results: The patient had a recent chest X-ray that showed mild radiation pneumonitis, which means inflammation of the lungs due to radiation.

Assessment: The patient has stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer and radiation pneumonitis.

Problem list: Nonsmall cell lung cancer; radiation pneumonitis; painful swallowing.

Plan:

Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a complaint of shortness of breath, which began over the weekend, and worsened to the point where she can no longer walk her dog down the street, a decrease from her usual daily mile-long walks. She reports a dry cough and painful swallowing, especially when swallowing, which is described as a sharp pain in the chest. She denies fever, body aches, and sore throat, but notes that swallowing is painful and does not feel like she has a cold. She has been experiencing some difficulty taking in liquids, but has been able to take Ensure and milkshakes. She reports feeling a little nauseated and tired after her last chemotherapy dose, but notes that she took zofran and got some rest, which helped. She mentions that her husband and daughter have been very supportive.

Past medical history: The patient has a recent diagnosis of stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer and is undergoing neo-adjuvant chemotherapy. She has a 4.4 cm left upper lobe nodule.

Review of systems: The patient reports some difficulty swallowing, but denies other symptoms.

Current medications: She is currently taking chemotherapy (sysplatin and a topicide) and has been prescribed zofran for nausea.

Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's pulse ox is normal, and her vitals are within normal limits.

Physical exam: The patient has a normal heart exam, with no cervical lymphadenopathy or subclavicular adenopathy. She has some crackles in her lungs bilaterally, which is expected due to her lung cancer diagnosis. Her skin exam reveals erythema on the anterior aspect of her chest on the left side, which could be due to radiation. Her extremities show no lower extremity edema.

Test Results: The patient had a recent chest x-ray, which showed mild radiation pneumonitis.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has stage three nonsmall cell lung cancer and is undergoing neo-adjuvant chemotherapy. She also has radiation pneumonitis and painful swallowing due to the radiation.

Problem list: Lung cancer; confirmed; Radiation pneumonitis; confirmed; Painful swallowing; confirmed.

Plan:
I prescribed prednisone 40mg orally daily for 5 days to reduce inflammation in her lungs and help her breathe better. I also prescribed lidocaine spray to help alleviate the pain when swallowing. I will continue her current chemotherapy regimen and radiation treatment.

Follow-up: We will see the patient back in two weeks to assess her progress and adjust her treatment plan as needed.
